items.q: What temperature range is the food 'danger zone'?
items.correct: 5 to 63 degrees Celsius - where most bacteria multiply rapidly.
items.why: Students assume danger = very hot. It's the MIDDLE range that's dangerous because bacteria thrive there. Fridges stay below 5; cooking exceeds 63 (often 75+ for safety). The middle is where they multiply.

items.q: What is the difference between a food allergy and an intolerance?
items.correct: An allergy involves the immune system; intolerance does not.
items.misconception: They are the same with different severity.
items.random_wrong: Allergies are temporary, intolerances are permanent.
items.why: Students treat these as severity points on one spectrum. They have different mechanisms - allergies trigger immune (IgE) responses (potentially fatal anaphylaxis); intolerances are digestive (e.g., lactase deficiency).

items.correct: Energy used to maintain basic bodily functions at complete rest.
items.why: Students think BMR is about exercise. It's the baseline energy needed just to STAY ALIVE - breathing, heart, brain, organ function - typically 60-70% of total daily energy use even before any activity.

items.q: Daily max for free sugars (g, adults)?
items.why: Correct answer: 30. “10” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's High. “Low” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is the daily salt limit in grams for adults?
items.why: Exact answer: 6. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
